overview: Brain teasers in investment banking interviews test three things: your ability to think under pressure, structure your reasoning clearly, and perform mental arithmetic accurately without a calculator. Interviewers are not looking for the 'right answer' — they are evaluating your cognitive process, composure, and intellectual honesty when faced with an unfamiliar problem. The questions fall into three categories: mental math and quantitative estimation, market sizing and Fermi problems, and logic puzzles or finance-specific riddles. Mastering all three requires practice with a consistent framework, not memorization of answers.

lessons.title: Mental Math and Estimation

blocks.text: Why Investment Banks Test Mental Math

In investment banking, you will regularly be in client meetings, deal calls, and IC presentations where a managing director turns to you and asks: 'What's the implied return if we pay $800M for this company and sell it for $1.3B in five years?' The answer needs to come in seconds, not minutes. Mental math ability signals quantitative competence, confidence under pressure, and genuine fluency with numbers — not just familiarity with Excel formulas. Banks test mental math to identify candidates who truly 'live and breathe' numbers.

There is also a practical dimension: investment banking involves enormous amounts of number-checking, sanity-testing, and cross-referencing financial data. Analysts who can quickly spot that a number is off by an order of magnitude — because their mental math is calibrated — save hours of debugging and prevent embarrassing errors in client deliverables.
The Rule of 72

The Rule of 72 is the single most useful mental math shortcut in finance. It states that the number of years required to double your money is approximately 72 divided by the annual interest rate (or growth rate). This works because ln(2) ≈ 0.693, and 72/rate ≈ ln(2)/ln(1+rate) for typical rates.
Compound Growth Shortcuts
Rather than computing exact compound growth for every problem, memorize these approximate anchors. They allow you to quickly estimate final values for standard scenarios that appear in interviews.

blocks.title: Key Anchors to Memorize
blocks.text: 10% growth x 7 years ≈ 2x. 15% growth x 5 years ≈ 2x. 20% growth x 4 years ≈ 2x (since 1.2^4 = 2.07). These are the most commonly tested scenarios. Also: 10% for 3 years ≈ 1.33x (not 1.30x — compound growth is always slightly more than simple growth).
Mental Math Tricks: Break, Round, Estimate

The 'Break, Round, Estimate' method is the core technique for on-the-spot arithmetic. Step 1 — Break: decompose hard numbers into easier components (e.g., $247M becomes $250M - $3M). Step 2 — Round: round to the nearest convenient number for fast computation. Step 3 — Estimate: perform the calculation on rounded numbers, then adjust for your rounding error. Always communicate your approach: 'I'll round $247M to $250M for simplicity, so the answer is approximately X.'

items: Multiplying by 11: Multiply by 10 and add the original. 43 x 11 = 430 + 43 = 473.
Multiplying large numbers: Break into factors. 35 x 24 = 35 x 20 + 35 x 4 = 700 + 140 = 840.
Percentage tricks: 15% of 80 = 10% of 80 + 5% of 80 = 8 + 4 = 12. Or: 15% of 80 = 80% of 15 = 12.
Division shortcuts: Dividing by 8 = dividing by 2 three times. 496 / 8 = 248 / 4 = 124 / 2 = 62.

blocks.text: Worked Practice Problems

blocks.title: Problem 1: Revenue Growth
blocks.text: Question: If revenues grow 15% for 3 years starting from $200M, what is the revenue? Solution: From the table, 15% x 3 years ≈ 1.52x (1.15^3 = 1.521). $200M x 1.521 = $304M. Quick check: Year 1: $200M x 1.15 = $230M. Year 2: $230M x 1.15 = $264.5M. Year 3: $264.5M x 1.15 = $304M. Answer: approximately $304M. In an interview, say: 'I know 1.15 cubed is approximately 1.52, so $200M grows to about $304M.'

blocks.title: Problem 2: Market Cap Calculation
blocks.text: Question: A company has a P/E ratio of 20x, EPS of $5, and 100 million shares outstanding. What is the market cap? Solution: Stock price = EPS x P/E = $5 x 20 = $100/share. Market cap = Price x Shares = $100 x 100M = $10 billion. This is a two-step problem: P/E to price, then price to market cap. Notice: EPS x P/E = Net Income / Shares x Price / EPS = Price. And Market Cap = Price x Shares = Net Income x P/E. So: Net Income = EPS x Shares = $5 x 100M = $500M. Market Cap = $500M x 20 = $10B. Always cross-check your answer.

blocks.title: Problem 3: EBITDA Projection
blocks.text: Question: EBITDA is $50M today, growing 10% per year. What is it in 5 years? Solution: From memory, 10% x 5 years = 1.61x. $50M x 1.61 = $80.5M. Round to approximately $80M. The exact answer is $50M x 1.1^5 = $50M x 1.6105 = $80.5M. For interviews, say: '$50M growing at 10% for 5 years — I know 10% growth for 5 years is roughly 1.61x, so EBITDA reaches about $80-81M.'

blocks.title: Problem 4: IRR Approximation
blocks.text: Question: A PE firm invests $100M and exits for $250M in 4 years. Approximate the IRR. Solution: MOIC = $250M / $100M = 2.5x. IRR = (2.5)^(1/4) - 1. From memory or the Rule of 72: at 25% IRR, money doubles every ~2.9 years (72/25 = 2.88). Two doublings in 5.8 years = 4x. But we need 2.5x in 4 years. A 4-year doubling implies 72/4 ≈ 18% rate = 2x. We need 2.5x, so the rate is between 18% and 25%. Answer: approximately 25-26% IRR. Exact: 2.5^0.25 - 1 = 1.257 - 1 = 25.7%. Your estimate was excellent.
Key Numbers to Memorize for Banking Interviews

items: US GDP: approximately $28 trillion (2024). GDP per capita: approximately $80,000.
US population: 335 million. Global population: 8.1 billion.
Apple market cap: ~$3 trillion (largest US company by market cap as of 2024).
US Federal Reserve target rate: 5.25-5.5% (peak 2023; cutting in 2024-2025).

keyTakeaways: The Rule of 72 is the most powerful mental math shortcut: years to double = 72 / annual return. Memorize common pairs (e.g., 8% = 9 years, 10% = 7.2 years).
Compound growth anchors: 10% x 5 years = 1.61x, 10% x 7 years = 2x, 15% x 5 years = 2x, 20% x 4 years = 2x.
Use Break-Round-Estimate for all mental arithmetic: state your rounding, compute on clean numbers, then adjust.
Market cap = Stock Price x Shares = Net Income x P/E. EBITDA x EV multiple = Enterprise Value. Equity Value = EV - Net Debt.
Memorize key macro numbers: US GDP ($28T), US population (335M), S&P 500 market cap (~$47T) — these anchor all estimation problems.
lessons.interviewTip: When doing mental math in an interview, always narrate your thinking: 'Let me approximate 1.15 cubed as about 1.52...' This does two things: it shows the interviewer your methodology (which they care about more than the exact answer) and it gives you a moment to think without awkward silence. If you make an arithmetic error and catch it, correct yourself calmly — interviewers appreciate intellectual honesty over false confidence.

lessons.title: Market Sizing and Fermi Estimation

blocks.text: What Market Sizing Tests

Market sizing questions — 'How large is the US coffee market?' 'What is the annual revenue of all dry cleaners in America?' — are not tests of obscure knowledge. Interviewers know you don't have this data memorized. They are testing three things: (1) your ability to structure an ambiguous problem into a logical framework, (2) your comfort making and stating explicit assumptions, and (3) your ability to arrive at a reasonable ballpark answer using arithmetic you can do in your head. The process matters far more than the precision of your answer.

Market sizing also reflects a real skill in investment banking: early in any M&A or ECM engagement, bankers need to quickly size the addressable market for a client's business to support the investment thesis or IPO narrative. A founder claiming a '$500 billion market opportunity' deserves scrutiny — and bankers who can sanity-check that number on the spot are genuinely valuable.
Two Frameworks: Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up

blocks.caption: Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Approach
rows: Start with total population or economy; narrow by relevant segments
Consumer markets with clear penetration rates
Start with unit economics (per store/customer/transaction); multiply by count
Requires knowing unit count, which itself needs estimation

blocks.text: For most problems, use both approaches if time permits and check whether they converge. If your top-down estimate is $50B and your bottom-up is $45B, you're in good shape. If they diverge dramatically ($50B vs. $5B), you've made an error in assumptions somewhere — find it.
Framework: The US Coffee Market

blocks.title: Worked Example: US Coffee Market
blocks.text: Top-Down Approach: US population: 335 million. Adults (18+): ~75% = 250 million. Coffee drinkers: ~65% of adults = 163 million drinkers. Average cups per day: 2 cups. Cups per year: 163M x 2 x 365 = 119 billion cups. At-home coffee cost: ~$0.25/cup (coffee beans/pods). Away-from-home (35% of cups): $4.00/cup. Blended average: 65% x $0.25 + 35% x $4.00 = $0.16 + $1.40 = $1.56/cup. Total market: 119 billion x $1.56 = approximately $186 billion. Bottom-Up Approach: Starbucks equivalent stores in US: ~50,000 (including all coffee shops). Average daily revenue per store: ~$10,000 (Starbucks averages ~$17,000; independent shops lower). Annual coffee shop revenue: 50,000 x $10,000 x 365 = $182 billion. Plus retail coffee (grocery/pods): ~$25 billion. Total: ~$207 billion. Convergence check: Both methods point to a $185-210B market. Triangulation gives confidence. State: 'I estimate the US coffee market at approximately $200 billion annually.'
Classic Fermi Questions

blocks.title: How Many Golf Balls Fit in a 747?
blocks.text: Step 1 — Volume of a 747: Interior cabin volume is approximately 876 cubic meters, but the usable space for packing is less — cargo hold and passenger cabin combined roughly 800 cubic meters. Convert: 800 m^3 = 800,000 liters = 800,000,000 cm^3. Step 2 — Volume of a golf ball: diameter ~4.3 cm, radius ~2.15 cm. Volume = (4/3) x pi x r^3 = 4.19 x (2.15)^3 = 4.19 x 9.94 ≈ 41.6 cm^3 per ball. Step 3 — Packing efficiency: Spheres pack at about 64% efficiency (random packing). Effective volume per ball: 41.6 / 0.64 ≈ 65 cm^3. Step 4 — Number of balls: 800,000,000 / 65 ≈ 12.3 million golf balls. State: 'Approximately 12 million golf balls.' Note: Various sources cite 10-25 million depending on assumptions — this range is acceptable.

blocks.title: How Many Piano Tuners Are in Chicago?
blocks.text: A Fermi estimation classic. Step 1 — Chicago population: approximately 2.7 million people; ~1 million households. Step 2 — Households with pianos: assume 5% of households own a piano = 50,000 pianos. Plus institutions (schools, churches, concert halls): approximately 10,000 pianos. Total pianos: ~60,000. Step 3 — Tuning frequency: pianos should be tuned twice a year = 120,000 tunings per year in Chicago. Step 4 — Tuner productivity: a piano tuner can tune 4-5 pianos per day. Working 250 days/year: 4.5 x 250 = 1,125 tunings per tuner per year. Step 5 — Number of tuners: 120,000 / 1,125 = approximately 107 tuners. Round to ~100 piano tuners in Chicago. (Actual number is approximately 125 — very close!)

blocks.title: Annual Revenue of a Manhattan McDonald's
blocks.text: Step 1 — Hours of operation: 18 hours/day (6am to midnight in most Manhattan locations). Step 2 — Customer throughput: Manhattan McDonald's are extremely busy. Rush hours: 3 hours x 100 customers/hour = 300 customers. Off-peak hours: 15 hours x 50 customers/hour = 750 customers. Total daily customers: ~1,050. Step 3 — Average ticket: $10-12 per order (combo meal prices). Use $11. Step 4 — Daily revenue: 1,050 x $11 = $11,550/day. Step 5 — Annual revenue: $11,550 x 365 = approximately $4.2 million. Sanity check: McDonald's top-volume US stores do $5-7M in annual revenue; busy Manhattan locations may hit $5M+. Our estimate of ~$4M is in the right ballpark.

blocks.title: Smartphone Market in India
blocks.text: Step 1 — India population: 1.44 billion. Addressable population (those who can afford smartphones): approximately 500 million (middle class and above). Step 2 — Current penetration: approximately 55-60% of addressable population already has a smartphone = 280 million existing smartphone users. Step 3 — Upgrade cycle: users upgrade every 2-3 years. New purchases: 280M / 2.5 years = 112M phones/year from upgrades. Step 4 — New adopters: remaining 220M without smartphones; 10-15% convert annually = 25M new users/year. Step 5 — Total annual volume: 112M + 25M = 137M phones/year. Step 6 — Average selling price: blended average across budget (~$100) and mid-range (~$250) and premium (~$800). India is budget-dominated: 70% budget, 25% mid, 5% premium. Blended ASP: 0.70 x $100 + 0.25 x $250 + 0.05 x $800 = $70 + $62.50 + $40 = $172.50. Step 7 — Market size: 137M x $172 = approximately $24 billion. Note: Actual India smartphone market in 2023-2024 was approximately $26-30B — very close!
How to Present Your Answer Clearly

Presentation is as important as the math. Follow this structure for every market sizing question: (1) Clarify the question — 'Just to confirm, we're sizing the US coffee market by annual retail and away-from-home revenue?' (2) State your approach — 'I'll use a top-down approach starting with the US adult population.' (3) Walk through assumptions explicitly — 'I'll assume 65% of adults are regular coffee drinkers and average 2 cups per day.' (4) Compute step by step, narrating each step. (5) State your final answer with appropriate confidence — 'My estimate is approximately $200 billion, with the caveat that I've assumed...'

blocks.title: Key Insight: Assumptions Drive Everything
blocks.text: The difference between a good and great market sizing answer is not the math — it's the quality and defensibility of your assumptions. Bad assumption: 'I'll assume 50% of people drink coffee.' Better assumption: 'According to the National Coffee Association, approximately 62% of US adults drink coffee daily, so I'll use 65% as my estimate.' Even if you don't know the statistic exactly, grounding your assumptions in logic or approximate real-world context makes your answer credible. Interviewers appreciate candidates who flag uncertainty: 'I'm less confident about the average ticket size here — I'd want to check that assumption.'

keyTakeaways: Market sizing tests structured thinking, clear assumptions, and mental arithmetic — the exact answer is secondary to the quality of the process.
Always use two approaches (top-down and bottom-up) when time permits and check for convergence — major divergence signals an error in assumptions.
State all assumptions explicitly before computing — interviewers evaluate whether your assumptions are reasonable, not just whether your math is correct.
Key anchors: US population 335M, India 1.44B, average US household income $75K, US GDP $28T — these ground virtually every market sizing problem.
Present in a structured narrative: clarify, state approach, walk through assumptions, compute step by step, state final answer with caveats.
lessons.interviewTip: Practice 5-10 market sizing problems out loud before interviews — the verbal narration is a skill that requires rehearsal separate from the math. Start with the US coffee market, piano tuners, and McDonald's revenue, then progress to trickier questions like 'estimate the total addressable market for a drone delivery service.' Time yourself to get under 3 minutes per answer. Interviewers in ECM, M&A, and PE all use market sizing to see if you can construct the 'TAM slide' for a pitch book under pressure.

lessons.title: Logic Puzzles and Finance Riddles

blocks.text: What Interviewers Test with Logic Questions

Logic puzzles and finance riddles in IB interviews serve a different purpose than market sizing. They test: (1) lateral thinking — can you step outside conventional approaches? (2) structured problem-solving — do you systematically eliminate possibilities? (3) comfort with ambiguity — do you panic or methodically work through uncertainty? (4) intellectual honesty — will you admit when you don't know something rather than guess randomly? These questions are especially common at Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and other bulge-bracket firms during early-round interviews.
Classic Logic Puzzles

blocks.title: Why Are Manhole Covers Round?
blocks.text: The famous Microsoft/investment banking brainteaser. There are multiple valid answers — give 2-3. (1) A round cover cannot fall through a round hole: a circle's diameter is constant in all directions, so a round lid cannot be accidentally dropped through its opening. A square or rectangular cover could be tilted and fall through diagonally. (2) Round covers are easier to transport and move: they can be rolled on their edge rather than carried flat. (3) Round covers don't need to be oriented: when replacing a circular cover, no alignment is required — any rotation works. (4) Round is stronger: circular shapes distribute stress evenly, making them more resistant to the point loads from vehicle traffic. Lead with answer (1) as it is most structurally compelling, then offer the others.

blocks.title: 12 Balls, One Heavier — 3 Weighings to Find It
blocks.text: Classic logic puzzle. Solution (a well-known systematic approach): Weighing 1: Divide 12 balls into three groups of 4. Weigh Group A (4 balls) vs. Group B (4 balls). Case 1 — They balance: the odd ball is in Group C (4 balls). Weighing 2: Take 3 balls from Group C, weigh against 3 known-good balls. If they balance: the 4th Group C ball is odd. If they don't: the heavier side has the odd ball. Weighing 3: If you need to distinguish among 3 possibilities, weigh two suspected balls against each other — the heavier one is the odd ball, or if they balance, the third is. Case 2 — Group A is heavier in Weighing 1: the odd ball is in Group A. Continue similar logic. Full solution is complex; the key interview skill is demonstrating systematic grouping and logic, not necessarily arriving at the full proof under pressure.

blocks.title: 100 Floors, 2 Eggs — Minimize Worst-Case Drops
blocks.text: Setup: You have 2 eggs and a 100-floor building. Determine the highest floor from which an egg can be dropped without breaking, using the minimum number of drops in the worst case. Naive approach (wrong): Drop first egg every 10 floors (10, 20, 30...). If it breaks at floor 50, you must test floors 41-49 with the second egg = 9 more drops. Worst case: 19 drops (if first egg breaks at floor 10 after testing floor 10 first, then need to test floors 1-9 = 9 more = 10 total; but if at floor 100 after 10 tests = 10 + 9 = 19). Optimal approach: Start at floor 14. If it breaks, use egg 2 to test floors 1-13 (13 drops). Worst case: 14. If it doesn't break, go to floor 27 (14+13). If it breaks, test floors 15-26 = 12 drops. Worst case: 2 + 12 = 14. Continue: 14, 27, 39, 50, 60, 69, 77, 84, 90, 95, 99, 100. The pattern: start at 14, increment decreasing by 1 each time (14, 13, 12...). Worst case: 14 drops. The interview answer: 'The optimal strategy starts the first egg at floor 14, reducing the step size by 1 each time, yielding a worst case of 14 drops.' Show the logic, not just the answer.
Finance-Specific Riddles

blocks.title: Revenue Doubles but Net Income Doesn't Change — What Happened?
blocks.text: Question: A company's revenue doubles from $100M to $200M, but net income stays flat at $10M. What could explain this? This tests your understanding of the income statement and cost structure. Multiple valid answers: (1) Costs doubled proportionally — the company scaled revenue but also doubled its cost base (COGS, SG&A, R&D), keeping absolute profit the same. (2) The business entered a new low-margin segment — the new $100M of revenue came from a business with nearly zero net margin, dragging blended margins down from 10% to 5%. (3) Interest expense doubled — the company borrowed heavily to fund growth, and interest payments consumed the additional operating profit. (4) Tax rate increased — potentially due to losing a tax benefit or entering higher-tax jurisdictions. (5) One-time charges in the current period — restructuring costs, impairments, or legal settlements offset the operating profit gain. In an interview, list 2-3 possibilities confidently and ask if the interviewer wants you to elaborate on any.

blocks.title: Share Buyback: What Happens to EPS, P/E, and ROE?
blocks.text: Question: A company buys back 10% of its shares outstanding. What happens to EPS, P/E, and ROE? EPS: Increases. Net income stays the same; shares outstanding decrease by 10%; EPS = Net Income / Shares goes up. If net income was $100M and shares were 100M (EPS = $1.00), after buyback of 10M shares: EPS = $100M / 90M = $1.11 — an 11% increase. P/E Ratio: In the short term, if the stock price doesn't change, P/E falls because EPS went up. But in practice, investors often bid the price up proportionally to reflect higher EPS, keeping P/E roughly constant. The company is also signaling confidence in its stock, which can further support the price. ROE (Return on Equity): Increases. Equity (book value) decreases because cash was used to buy back shares — cash leaves the balance sheet, reducing assets and equity. Smaller equity base with same net income = higher ROE. Example: Net Income $100M, Equity $500M (ROE = 20%). After $50M buyback: Equity = $450M. ROE = $100M / $450M = 22.2%. Be careful: if the buyback was funded by debt, the analysis changes — interest expense goes up, which reduces net income.

blocks.title: LBO: EBITDA Goes Up $10M — How Does Equity Value Change?
blocks.text: Question: In an LBO context, EBITDA increases by $10M. How does equity value change? Framework: Equity Value = Enterprise Value - Net Debt. Enterprise Value = EBITDA x Multiple. Key insight: Net Debt is unchanged (debt doesn't change because EBITDA went up). So all the EV increase flows to equity. Calculation: If exit multiple is 10x, a $10M EBITDA increase creates $10M x 10 = $100M of additional enterprise value. Since net debt is unchanged, equity value increases by $100M. This is the power of EBITDA growth in an LBO — it creates leveraged upside for equity. For comparison: if the exit multiple were 8x, the same $10M EBITDA increase creates $80M of equity value. This is why PE firms obsess over EBITDA improvement — each dollar of EBITDA created is worth multiple dollars of equity at exit.

blocks.title: If Interest Rates Rise, What Happens to Bond Prices, DCF Values, and P/E Multiples?
blocks.text: This is one of the most fundamental macro finance questions. Bond Prices: Fall. Existing bonds have fixed coupons. When market rates rise, new bonds offer higher yields, making existing bonds less attractive. Their prices must fall until their yield equals the new market rate. A bond with a 4% coupon becomes unattractive when new bonds offer 6% — investors will only buy it at a discount to par. DCF Values: Fall. In a DCF, future cash flows are discounted at the WACC (which includes the risk-free rate). Higher rates increase the WACC (higher risk-free rate = higher cost of equity and debt). Higher discount rate = lower present value of future cash flows = lower DCF value. Growth companies (with more cash flows far in the future) are hurt more than mature companies. P/E Multiples: Tend to fall. The theoretical P/E multiple is 1/(Cost of Equity - Growth). Higher rates increase the cost of equity, compressing the justified P/E. Additionally, higher rates increase borrowing costs, reduce corporate earnings, and make bonds more competitive vs. equities, all of which compress multiples. This is what happened in 2022: the Fed hiked rates aggressively, bond prices fell, stocks fell (especially growth stocks), and P/E multiples compressed significantly.
Framework for Unknown Questions

When confronted with an unfamiliar brain teaser or logic puzzle, use the following structure: (1) Pause — don't panic or blurt out a random answer; take 5-10 seconds to think; (2) State your assumptions — 'I'll assume the question is asking about X'; (3) Break the problem into components — 'Let me think about this in three parts...'; (4) Work through each component systematically; (5) Arrive at a range or answer with a confidence interval — 'Based on my analysis, I believe the answer is approximately X, though I'm less confident about assumption Y'; (6) Ask for feedback — 'Does my approach make sense? Would you like me to revisit any assumption?'
Handling 'I Don't Know' Gracefully

One of the most common mistakes in IB interviews is guessing randomly when faced with an unknown. A better approach: 'I'm not immediately sure, but let me think through it logically...' then attempt the problem. If you are genuinely stuck after a genuine attempt: 'I'm not certain of the exact answer, but here's how I would approach finding it: I would [describe your research/reasoning process].' This demonstrates intellectual honesty — a quality top banks specifically seek. The worst answer is a confidently stated wrong answer. The second-worst is silence.

blocks.title: Key Insight: What Interviewers Are Really Looking For
blocks.text: A Goldman Sachs interviewer once explained: 'We give brainteasers not because the answer matters, but because they reveal character under pressure. Does the candidate panic? Do they make random guesses? Do they think systematically? Are they intellectually curious or do they just want to move past the discomfort? We can teach someone the right answer to any specific question. We cannot teach them to think clearly under pressure — that's what we're selecting for.' Process, calmness, and intellectual honesty win over a memorized correct answer every time.

blocks.title: Pre-Interview Preparation Checklist
blocks.text: Practice these 10 finance riddles before any IB interview: (1) Revenue doubles, NI flat — what happened? (2) Share buyback — EPS, P/E, ROE effects. (3) EBITDA up $10M in an LBO — equity value change. (4) Interest rates rise — bond prices, DCF, P/E. (5) Enterprise value vs. equity value — when to use each. (6) Working capital increases — cash flow impact. (7) Deferred revenue increases — revenue impact. (8) Depreciation is a non-cash charge — why does it matter for DCF? (9) Company has $100M net income but $0 free cash flow — why? (10) Capex vs. Opex — income statement and tax implications. These 10 questions cover the essential finance conceptual territory tested in 90% of IB interviews.

keyTakeaways: Logic puzzles test systematic thinking and intellectual honesty under pressure — the process matters more than arriving at the exact correct answer.
For 'manhole cover'-style questions, give 2-3 distinct answers (structural integrity, rollability, no orientation needed) rather than a single rote response.
Finance riddles (share buyback, EBITDA in LBO, interest rates) test mechanistic understanding of the income statement, balance sheet, and valuation — not memorized answers.
When genuinely stuck, verbalize your approach: 'I'm not certain, but I would reason through it by...' — intellectual honesty is valued over confident wrong answers.
Memorize the macro finance effects of rising rates: bond prices fall, DCF values fall, P/E multiples compress — this chain of logic underpins dozens of interview questions.
lessons.interviewTip: For any finance riddle involving a change in one metric, always trace the full path through the three financial statements: income statement first, then balance sheet, then cash flow statement. Ask yourself: 'What does this change do to revenue, EBITDA, EBIT, net income? How does it change assets, liabilities, equity? How does it affect operating, investing, and financing cash flows?' This systematic approach ensures you don't miss second-order effects (e.g., a buyback funded by debt affects both the equity and liabilities sides of the balance sheet, and adds interest expense to the income statement).
